name: Saudi Arabia
overview: Saudi Arabia's Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL), issued by Royal Decree M/19 of 2021 and substantially amended in 2023, has applied since September 2023 with enforcement following a one-year compliance grace period ending September 2024. SDAIA supervises the regime and has issued extensive implementing rules, transfer regulations and a national data governance platform for registrations and DPO notifications.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection Law (Royal Decree M/19 of 1443H, as amended by M/148)
legal_instruments.summary: Comprehensive law: lawful bases including a 2023-added legitimate-interest basis (non-sensitive data), data subject rights, controller registration obligations, breach notification to SDAIA (72 hours), and criminal penalties for sensitive-data disclosure violations.

legal_instruments.title: Implementing Regulation of the Personal Data Protection Law
legal_instruments.summary: Detailed rules on consent, rights procedures, DPO appointment criteria, DPIAs, security controls and breach notification content; updated September 2024.

legal_instruments.title: Regulation on Personal Data Transfer Outside the Kingdom
legal_instruments.summary: Amended transfer regulation: transfers permitted under adequacy assessments, or safeguards (SCCs, BCRs, certification) with risk assessments for certain cases; removed the earlier strict-localisation default.

developments.title: PDPL enforcement begins after grace period
developments.summary: The one-year compliance window closed, making the PDPL fully enforceable; SDAIA simultaneously issued updated implementing and transfer regulations and opened the National Data Governance Platform registration.

developments.title: SDAIA expands PDPL guidance suite
developments.summary: SDAIA continued releasing guidance (DPO, DPIA, consent, disclosure cases) and signalled the first adequacy/whitelist assessments for cross-border transfers.

penalties: Warnings and fines up to SAR 5 million per violation (doubling for repeats) via SDAIA committee; criminal penalty up to 2 years' imprisonment and/or SAR 3 million for unlawful disclosure of sensitive data.
cross_border: Transfers allowed to jurisdictions with adequate protection (SDAIA assessments) or with appropriate safeguards (SCCs, BCRs, certification) plus risk assessment; specific statutory purposes required.
